title: 15-Minute Garlic Butter Pasta

subs.name: any dry Indian vermicelli (semiya)
subs.notes: cook only 3 minutes — check frequently to avoid overcooking

subs.name: olive oil (3 tbsp) for a dairy-free version
subs.notes: classic Italian aglio e olio — equally authentic

ingredients.name: red chilli powder or chilli flakes

subs.name: nutritional yeast for a vegan finish

steps.text: Bring a large pot of well-salted water to a rolling boil. Cook the pasta according to packet directions until just al dente — with a tiny firm centre. Before draining, scoop out half a cup of the starchy pasta water.

While the pasta cooks, melt butter in a wide kadai or frying pan on medium-low. Add garlic slices and cook very slowly, stirring, until the garlic turns light golden and fragrant — about 3 minutes. Do not let it brown too darkly or it will turn bitter.
Add black pepper and chilli flakes to the butter and stir 15 seconds so the spices bloom in the fat.

Add the drained pasta directly to the pan. Pour in 4 tablespoons of the reserved pasta water and toss vigorously on medium heat for 1–2 minutes. The starchy water emulsifies with the butter to form a glossy, clinging sauce.
Remove from heat and toss in grated cheese if using. Taste for salt — the pasta water brings salt, so add cautiously. Scatter chopped parsley or coriander over and serve immediately.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: butter → olive oil (3 tbsp) for a dairy-free version (classic Italian aglio e olio — equally authentic); cheese → nutritional yeast for a vegan finish (adds a savoury, nutty note); pasta → any dry Indian vermicelli (semiya) (cook only 3 minutes — check frequently to avoid overcooking)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook pasta in a heavy-bottomed pot or pressure-cooker without the lid on medium-high — no specialised equipment needed. Any neutral oil or butter available at Indian grocery stores works for the sauce. If parmesan is unavailable, grated Amul processed cheese melts beautifully into the pasta and is sold everywhere.
